---
title: "Differential Gene Expression For Breast Cancer"
author: "Rushik S"
date: "2026-02-17"
output: html_document
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.


Get the libraries
```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```


```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(GEOquery)
```

Read data using readr
```{r}
library(readr)
data <- read_csv("GSE183947_fpkm.csv.gz")
dim(data)
```



Get the metadata using GEOquery
```{r}
gse <- getGEO(GEO='GSE183947', GSEMatrix=TRUE)
gse

```
Get the phenodata andto put in the metadata  variables
```{r}
metadata <- pData(phenoData(gse[[1]]))
head(metadata)
```

Get our title, 
Get characteristics_ch1 to determine whether the sample comes from tissue or tumor
Get characteristics_ch1.1 column for metastasis: yes or metastasis: no

```{r}
metadata.subset <- select(metadata, c("title", "characteristics_ch1", "characteristics_ch1.1", "description"))

head(metadata.subset)
```
Modify the column names for better accessibility using the pipe operator from tidyverse (%>%).

Notice that characteristics_ch1 column has "tissue: breast tumor". Remove "tissue: " part of each row to shorten the values.

Remove "metastasis: " part from each row of characteristics_ch1.1 as well using gsub().


```{r}
metadata.modified <- metadata %>%
  select("title", "characteristics_ch1", "characteristics_ch1.1", "description") %>%
  rename(tissue=characteristics_ch1) %>%
  rename(metastasis=characteristics_ch1.1) %>%
  mutate(tissue=gsub("tissue: ", "", tissue)) %>%
  mutate(metastasis=gsub("metastasis: ", "", metastasis))
metadata.modified
```

Now join the metadata with our gene expression data
Problem: Each column in the data corresponds to our sample. However, each row in the metadata.modified corresponds to a sample. So we have a dataset that uses sample numbers as columns and we have another dataset that uses sample numbers as rows.

Solution: We fix this through reshaping our data
gather converts from wide to long format

This basically means.
each gene gets its own row for every sample.

Before:
        Sample 1    Sample 2    Sample 3
Gene A      10        4           2
Gene B      10        5           2
Gene C      10        6           1

After gather():
Sample 1 Gene A 10
Sample 1 Gene B 10
Sample 1 Gene C 10
Sample 2 Gene A 4
Sample 2 Gene B 5
Sample 2 Gene C 6
Sample 3 Gene A 2
Sample 3 Gene B 2
Sample 3 Gene C 1

```{r}
names(data)[1] = "X"
data.long <- data %>%
  rename(gene=X) %>%
  gather(key='samples', value='FPKM', -gene)

# head(data.long)
tail(data.long)
```

Now let's join data.long with metadata.modified.
So we are performing a left join on data.long and metadata.modified on the the sample number.
We have "samples" in data and "description" in the metadata.

What is a left join?
We grab rows that are in both data.long and metadata.modified and we also grab rows that are exclusively in data.long
```{r}
data.long <- data.long %>%
  left_join(., metadata.modified, by = c("samples"="description"))
# head(data.long)
tail(data.long)
```

Basic exploration of data using aggregate functions

```{r}
data.long %>% 
  filter(gene == "BRCA1" | gene == "BRCA2") %>%
  group_by(gene,  tissue) %>%
  summarise(mean_FPKM = mean(FPKM))
```
## Exploring the expression of MLIP, HMCN2, LRRN4CL, DUOX2
```{r}
data.long %>% 
  filter(gene == "GALNTL5" | gene == "MLIP" | gene == "HMCN2" | gene == "LRRN4CL" | gene == "DUOX2") %>%
  group_by(gene,  tissue) %>%
  summarise(mean_FPKM = mean(FPKM))
```



It can be seen that there is higher expression of BRCA1 in the tumor and lower expression of BRCA2 in the tumor.

## Ploting Gene expression data
Basic Format
ggplot(data, aes(x=variable1, y=variable2))  + 
  geom_bar()

### Charting a barplot
```{r}
library(ggplot2)
barplot <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA1") %>%
  head(60) %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = samples, y = FPKM, fill=tissue)) +
  geom_col()

ggsave("BRCA1 gene expression.png", barplot)
```

### Density
```{r}
density_plot <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA1") %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = FPKM, fill = tissue)) +
  geom_density()
ggsave("density_plot.png", density_plot)
```

### Box Plot
```{r}
box_plot <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA1") %>%
  filter(tissue == "breast tumor") %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = metastasis, y = FPKM)) +
  geom_boxplot()
ggsave("metastasis.png", box_plot)
```

```{r}
violin_plot <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA2") %>%
  filter(tissue == "breast tumor") %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = metastasis, y = FPKM)) +
  geom_violin()
ggsave('metastatsis vs BRCA2 expresion.png', violin_plot)
```

### Scatter Plot for BRCA1 and BRCA2 expression in Tumor
```{r}
scatter_plot <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA1" | gene == "BRCA2") %>%
  filter(tissue == "breast tumor") %>%
  spread(key = gene, value = FPKM) %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = BRCA1, y = BRCA2)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
ggsave("BRCA1 vs BRCA2 expression for tumors.png", scatter_plot)
scatter_plot
```

### Scatter Plot for BRCA1 and BRCA2 expression in Healthy Tissue
```{r}
scatter_plot_2 <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA1" | gene == "BRCA2") %>%
  filter(tissue != "breast tumor") %>%
  spread(key = gene, value = FPKM) %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = BRCA1, y = BRCA2)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
ggsave("BRCA1 vs BRCA2 expression for healthy tissue.png", scatter_plot_2)
scatter_plot_2
```

### Side by Side Comparision of BRCA1 vs BRCA2 expression in Healthy and Tumors

```{r}
scatter_plot_3 <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene == "BRCA1" | gene == "BRCA2") %>%
  spread(key = gene, value = FPKM) %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = BRCA1, y = BRCA2, color = tissue)) + 
  geom_point() + 
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE)
ggsave("BRCA1 vs BRCA2 expression for both sample types.png", scatter_plot_3)
scatter_plot_3
```

### Heatmap
```{r}
# genes_of_interest <- c(data %>%
#   select(X) %>%
#   slice(1:50))
# genes_of_interest

genes_of_interest <- c("BRCA1", "BRCA2", "TP53", "ALK", "MYCN",  "TSPAN6","TNMD",  "DPM1",  "SCYL3", "C1orf112", "FGR",
 "CFH","FUCA2", "GCLC", "NFYA", "STPG1", "NIPAL3",
"LAS1L","ENPP4","SEMA3F", "CFTR", "ANKIB1","CYP51A1",
 "KRIT1","RAD52", "BAD", "LAP3", "CD99",  "HS3ST1",  
 "AOC1",  "WNT16", "HECW1", "MAD1L1","LASP1", "SNX11",
 "TMEM176A", "M6PR",  "KLHL13","CYP26B1", "ICA1",  "DBNDD1",
 "ALS2",  "CASP10","CFLAR", "TFPI",  "NDUFAF7", "RBM5",
 "MTMR7", "SLC7A2","ARF5",  "SARM1", "POLDIP2", "PLXND1",  
 "AK2", "CD38")

genes_of_progression <- c("GALNTL5", "MLIP", "HMCN2", "LRRN4CL", "DUOX2")
plot_data_log <- log2(data.long$FPKM + 1)

heatmap <- data.long %>%
  filter(gene %in% genes_of_progression) %>%
  filter(log2(FPKM + 1) != 0) %>%
  arrange(tissue) %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = samples, y = gene, fill = log2(FPKM + 1), color = tissue)) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "red")

ggsave("heatmap.png", heatmap)
heatmap

```
```{r}
heatmap2 <- data.long %>%
  arrange(., metastasis) %>%
  filter(gene %in% genes_of_progression) %>%
  ggplot(., aes(x = samples, y = gene, fill = log2(FPKM + 1))) +
  geom_tile() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "white", high = "blue")
heatmap2
```

## Identify Differentially expressed genes using DESeq
```{r}
library(DESeq2)
```

### Prepare data
```{r}
# In our metadata, we have each row corresponding to a Sample number
# We get the description the column to access all sample numbers in metadata
colData <- metadata.modified$description

# In our data, we have each row corresponding to a gene and each column corresponds to a sample 
# We get the column names  to access all sample numbers in data
countsData <- data
countsData <- colnames(countsData[2:61])

# Check if sample numbers from both tables are matching
all(countsData %in% colData)

# Check if the sample numbers from both tables are in the same order
all(countsData == colData)

# We are good to go if both return TRUE
```

```{r}
library(limma)

# 1. Log-transform your data if it isn't already
# (Assuming 'data' is your normalized matrix)
newData <- data[1:61]
rownames(newData) <- data$X


log_counts <- log2(newData[2:61] + 1)
newData
log_counts

# 2. Create your design matrix
newMetadata = metadata.modified
rownames(newMetadata) = newMetadata$description
design <- model.matrix(~ tissue, data = newMetadata)

# 3. Fit the model
rownames(log_counts) <- data$X
fit <- lmFit(log_counts, design)
fit <- eBayes(fit, trend = TRUE) # Crucial: trend=TRUE for normalized data

# 4. Get results
results <- topTable(fit, coef = 2)
results
```
```{r}
# Get the top 50 genes by adjusted p-value
top_genes_table <- topTable(fit, coef = 2, number = 30)

# Get the names/IDs of these genes
top_gene_ids <- rownames(top_genes_table)
top_gene_ids
```

```{r}
# Subset your original log-transformed matrix to just these 50 genes
# Assuming 'log_counts' was your input to lmFit
plot_data <- log_counts[top_gene_ids, ]
library(pheatmap)

# 1. Setup column annotations (Metadata)
# Make sure the rownames of annotation_col match the colnames of plot_data
annotation_col <- newMetadata %>% 
  select(tissue, metastasis) 

# 2. Plot
top30heatmap <- pheatmap(plot_data, 
  annotation_col = annotation_col,
  scale = "row", # Centers each gene's expression at zero
  clustering_distance_rows = "correlation",
  clustering_distance_cols = "euclidean",
  show_colnames = FALSE,     # Clean up the bottom axis
  main = "Limma Top 30: Gene Expression Profile",
  color = colorRampPalette(c("navy", "white", "firebrick3"))(100)
)
ggsave("Top 30 Differentially Expressed Genes.png", top30heatmap)
```

### Exploring gene expression data with other genes of interest
```{r}
data.long %>% 
  filter(gene == "DEFB130" | gene == "UGT2A1" | gene == "KLK9" | gene == "CCDC177" | gene == "LCN6") %>%
  group_by(gene,  tissue) %>%
  summarise(mean_FPKM = mean(FPKM))
```
